verdict: Your ideal client — a busy founder who knows they need video to grow — lands on a site with strong proof, real numbers, and clear pricing if they dig for it. The reason they still might not enquire is that your homepage tries to speak to three very different buyers at once, so nobody feels it was written for them.
scope_note: A quick note on what this covers. This is a look at your strategy, messaging and content — how the site reads, what it asks visitors to do, and whether it turns a curious visitor into a booked call. Design is its own conversation and one that's much better had on a call, where I can ask about your clients, your goals and what feels right for you. If you'd like to have that chat, I'd genuinely love to.

fixes.title: Your homepage is trying to talk to three different people — none of them feel spoken to directly
fixes.detail: Within the first two scrolls, you're talking to founder-led small businesses, then creative agencies, then sport brands. Each of those people has different problems, different budgets and different decisions to make. A small business owner who arrives sees agency work and thinks 'this person is probably too big for me'; an agency producer sees SME content and thinks the opposite.
fixes.action: Pick the one audience you most want to win, rewrite the homepage entirely for them, and move the other two to dedicated sub-pages linked from the nav.

fixes.title: Your pricing exists and is genuinely clear — but a first-time visitor has no idea until they click
fixes.detail: Your Pricing page (once you find it) does exactly the right thing — £400/day, £800/day, £600-2,000 retainers, all spelled out. The problem is a visitor on the homepage sees none of this, and if they don't click, they assume 'probably expensive' and move on. That's avoidable.
fixes.action: Put a 'from £400/day' or 'packages from £600' signal directly on the homepage so visitors self-qualify before they reach for the back button.

fixes.title: There's no way to book you without picking up the phone or filling in a form — and plenty of people won't do either at 9pm on a Tuesday
fixes.detail: Every call-to-action across the site is 'Call Anoop' or a contact form. A founder watching your showreel at 9pm who'd happily book a 20-minute intro call right now can't, because there's no booking tool. They tell themselves they'll do it tomorrow. Most of them won't.
fixes.action: Add a Calendly (or similar) link to every main call-to-action — 'Book a 20-minute call' — so a visitor who's ready can commit to a time without waiting for you to call them back.

fixes.title: You have no independent reviews online — for a personal service, that's a real trust gap
fixes.detail: A cautious buyer about to spend £800+ on someone they've never met will search your name. Right now that returns your LinkedIn, Facebook and Instagram — no Google Business Profile reviews, no Trustpilot, nothing they can verify independently. Your on-site testimonials are excellent, but a prospect doesn't know you didn't hand-pick them.
fixes.action: Set up a Google Business Profile and message Rai, Aliraza and Neil asking if they'd leave a short review — three real Google reviews is enough to start.

fixes.title: Visitors who aren't ready to call you today have no way to stay in your orbit
fixes.detail: Someone lands on your site, likes what they see, but isn't ready — maybe they've got a shoot in 6 months, or they're still building their budget. Right now they leave and forget about you. There's nothing for them to take away, no email list, no guide, no reason to come back.
fixes.action: Create one simple lead magnet — a short PDF like 'The 5 videos every small business should have on their website' — and offer it in exchange for an email address on the homepage.

findings.check: One clear next step on every section
findings.note: You've got 'Call Anoop' buttons, a contact form, WhatsApp, and 'Book a Call' links all on the same page. Every option on its own is fine — stacked together, visitors get decision fatigue and pick nothing.
findings.rec: Pick one primary action per section (ideally a booking link) and demote the others to secondary text.

findings.check: Getting in touch is genuinely easy
findings.note: You've got a working contact form and WhatsApp, which is solid. What's missing is a booking tool so someone ready to commit to a time can do it immediately rather than waiting for you to call back.
findings.rec: Add Calendly or a similar booking link so visitors can self-book a 20-minute intro call.

findings.check: 3 clicks or fewer from landing to enquiring
findings.note: Your contact form is on the homepage itself, and there's a direct Contact page in the nav. A visitor who wants to get in touch can do it in one click.

findings.check: Main call to action AND a lower-commitment option
findings.note: Every action on your site asks someone to start a conversation with you. There's nothing for the 80% of visitors who like what they see but aren't ready to talk yet — no guide, no email list, no way to stay connected.
findings.rec: Add one simple lead magnet (a short PDF or video) that someone can download in exchange for their email.

findings.check: Clear what happens after someone gets in touch
findings.note: Your contact page spells it out well — fill in the form and you'll call them, and it even tells them what to share (goal, dates, budget) so they're prepared. That removes real uncertainty.

findings.check: Navigation set up for first-time visitors
findings.note: Your nav is clean and logical — Home, Work, Pricing, Contact. The small cart icon in the top-left (a Squarespace default) implies you sell something when you don't, which can briefly confuse first-time visitors.
findings.rec: Hide the cart icon from your Squarespace settings so the nav isn't suggesting a shop that doesn't exist.

findings.check: The page builds a case from start to finish
findings.note: Your homepage moves through problem → testimonial → case studies → process → contact, which is a solid structure. The disjointedness comes from jumping between three audiences (see Standing Out), which breaks the narrative flow.

findings.check: Clear differentiator
findings.note: 'Commercially-savvy' is a real differentiator in concept — a videographer who thinks about ROI, not just craft. Your case study numbers back it up. The phrase itself isn't very sticky though, and doesn't quite land as a memorable reason to choose you.
findings.rec: Sharpen the differentiator into a single concrete claim — e.g. 'Video built to perform, not just look good' — and lead with it.

findings.check: Specialist, not generalist
findings.note: Your homepage speaks to SME founders, creative agencies AND sport brands — three very different buyers with very different problems. A specialist picks one and owns it; serving three makes you look like a generalist who takes whatever work comes in.
findings.rec: Pick your main audience for the homepage and move the others to dedicated sub-pages.

findings.check: Offer clarity
findings.note: Your Events page (£999 Hero Film) and Pricing page (£400/£800 day rates, £600-2,000 retainers) both do this well — someone can work out what they'd get and what it'd cost. The gap is the homepage, where none of this shows.
findings.rec: Surface the package names and 'from' prices on the homepage so visitors see them before they decide to enquire.

findings.check: Why this business over the alternatives
findings.note: Your 9 years of experience and cross-sector credibility (NatWest, Heinz, TikTok, Microsoft) are genuinely strong. What's missing is the explicit comparison — why you over a cheaper local freelancer, or a production company.

findings.check: Honest about challenges and limitations
findings.note: There's no 'who this isn't right for' anywhere on the site. Businesses that only publish positives signal they'll say yes to anyone — which makes premium buyers hesitate.
findings.rec: Add a short 'who I'm not the right fit for' section on the About page (e.g. 'budgets under £X', 'one-off family events').

findings.check: Reflects current business
findings.note: The case studies reference 2025 shoots, the pricing is published, the Events offer is specific and current. The site feels actively maintained.

findings.check: Opens in the client's world, not the business's
findings.note: The first line on your homepage is 'Grow Your Business by Attracting More Customers' — immediately about the visitor's goal, not your craft. That's the right instinct.

findings.check: The core problem the client faces is named clearly
findings.note: 'You know you should be posting more content... but you're too busy running your business' is specific and real. It drifts into business-speak ('social proof', 'sales funnel') which makes the problem feel more abstract than it needs to.
findings.rec: Rewrite the problem section in the exact words a small business owner would use at the pub — not marketing language.

findings.check: Opening headline connects to the client's desire or situation
findings.note: 'Grow Your Business by Attracting More Customers' reads like the outcome the ideal client actually wants. It passes the 'that's exactly me' test.

findings.check: The emotional frustration is addressed
findings.note: You name the 'too busy running your business to manage social media marketing' feeling directly. That's the emotional driver, not just the practical problem.

findings.check: The bigger picture is described
findings.note: You mention 'struggling to differentiate yourself from competitors' which hints at the stakes. It doesn't quite land the bigger picture — what happens to the business in 12 months if this doesn't get sorted.
findings.rec: Add one line on what's actually at stake — e.g. 'competitors with better content take the customers you should be winning'.

findings.check: Site understands the client before selling anything
findings.note: You open with problem and empathy, then move to case studies and process. Credentials don't come first — that's the right order.

findings.check: What life looks like after working with you is described
findings.note: The 'after' is kept abstract — 'grow your brand', 'attract more customers'. A small business owner can't picture that. They need something concrete, like 'a month of content filmed in one day, ready to post'.
findings.rec: Describe what the business owner's week looks like after working with you — concrete, not aspirational language.

findings.check: There's a reason to act now
findings.note: Your contact page mentions 'Spaces for new campaigns are limited each quarter' which is a real reason to act. That same urgency isn't on the homepage, where most visitors actually decide.

findings.check: Clear within 5 seconds what you do and who for
findings.note: Inside five seconds you can tell this is a videographer who makes content for businesses who want to grow. That's the comprehension bar cleared.

findings.check: Headings pull the reader forward
findings.note: The case study sub-headings are strong — '+650,000 organic views', '300% increase in Heinz UK's TikTok following' pull the eye. Other headings are plain labels ('My Process for Making Videos', 'Who I Help').
findings.rec: Rewrite the plain-label headings to tease what's underneath, e.g. 'How I turn one shoot day into a month of content'.

findings.check: Copy is written about the client, not the business
findings.note: The homepage consistently uses 'you/your' and leads with the client's situation. The About page is naturally more 'I' heavy, which is fine for that page.

findings.check: Services describe what the client gets, not just what they're called
findings.note: The Hero Film description is great — specific, outcome-led. The SME and agency offerings aren't described the same way on the homepage, so a visitor can't tell what they'd actually receive.
findings.rec: Write a one-paragraph outcome description for each service (like the Hero Film one) and put them on the homepage.

findings.check: No empty claims that any competitor could also make
findings.note: 'Commercially-savvy', 'results-first mindset', 'strategic partner' and 'social-first videographer' are all unprovable claims any videographer could write. The case study numbers prove your work — the adjective-stacking doesn't.
findings.rec: Cut the empty adjectives and let the numbers from your case studies do the work.

findings.check: Long copy is genuinely scannable
findings.note: Short paragraphs, clear section breaks, plenty of visual breathing room. A business owner can scan the page on their phone and pick up the message.

findings.check: Testimonials are specific, named, and show a real result
findings.note: Rai Atwal (Big Fat Indian Pop Up), Neil Welch (SPORTbible), Aliraza Ali (Klickstart), Damien Trainor — all named with their business or role, and some are delivered on video. That's the gold standard.

findings.check: At least one case study with a real, measurable outcome
findings.note: Somatic School (30% CPA reduction, 6X ad creative volume), Heinz (300% TikTok growth), Building Materials Nationwide (120% brand awareness in 30 days) — multiple case studies with proper numbers. Very strong.

findings.check: Logo strips and badges show genuine proof
findings.note: NatWest, Heinz, Microsoft, TikTok, Body Shop, Zalando, Indeed — real brands you've genuinely contributed to (via agencies). The context is clear from the case studies.

findings.check: The About page makes the owner human and connects to why
findings.note: The About page copy is warm and personable, but there's no photo of you on it — visitors don't get to see the person they'd be hiring. That's a trust gap on a very personal service.
findings.rec: Add a real photo of yourself to the About page (and ideally the homepage too).

findings.check: Qualifications and credentials are visible
findings.note: '9+ years experience' appears repeatedly and is backed up by the work. There's no formal training, certification or membership of a body that might exist — worth adding if you have any.
findings.rec: If you have any industry memberships (BECTU, RPS, etc.) or formal training, surface them on the About page.

findings.check: Proof sits near the point where a decision is made
findings.note: Testimonials and case studies sit right alongside the 'Call Anoop' buttons on the homepage. Trust signals are where the decisions happen.

findings.check: Reviews and independent proof from outside the site
findings.note: A search for your name returns LinkedIn, Facebook and Instagram — no Google Business Profile reviews, no Trustpilot, nothing a cautious buyer can verify independently. For a personal service, that's a meaningful gap.
findings.rec: Set up a Google Business Profile and ask your past clients (Rai, Aliraza, Neil) to leave a Google review.

findings.check: Pricing is addressed openly
findings.note: Your Pricing page is genuinely clear — £400/day filming and editing, £800/day with lighting, £600-2,000 social packages. That's exactly the openness most videographer sites avoid.

findings.check: Visitor can work out whether this is right for them
findings.note: The About page 'Who I Help' section (Agencies, SMEs, Events) is useful. What's missing is who it's NOT for — a visitor with a £200 budget or a one-off family event can't self-select out.
findings.rec: Add a short 'who I'm not the right fit for' line to the About page.

findings.check: Real, recent articles a visitor can learn from
findings.note: There's no blog, no articles, no written content a visitor can read to get pre-qualified. The only path to learn about you is watching videos or calling you directly.
findings.rec: Start with three articles answering the real questions SMB owners ask before hiring a videographer — even 600 words each is enough.

findings.check: Content covers the topics buyers actually search for
findings.note: N/A — cross-reference the previous finding. No content means no topics covered.
findings.rec: Once articles exist, make sure they cover cost, comparisons (videographer vs in-house vs agency), and 'best videos to have on a small business website'.

findings.check: FAQs answer the real questions buyers have
findings.note: There are no FAQs on the site. Every basic question ('how long does a shoot take?', 'what do I need to prepare?', 'what if I don't like the edits?') gets deferred to a phone call.
findings.rec: Add an FAQ section to the homepage or a dedicated page answering the top 8 questions you get on every first call.

findings.check: Video content present
findings.note: This is the standout strength of the site. Showreels, case study videos, client testimonial videos — there's more than enough video content to build real trust, and that's exactly what you'd expect from a videographer.

findings.check: Path for a visitor to get ready before contact
findings.note: There's no 'before we talk' page, no recommended reading, no short guide to help a prospect arrive at the first call already informed. Most of your first calls end up being basic education that could happen before the call.
findings.rec: Create a simple 'before our call' page with 3-4 things to think about (goal, timeline, budget, examples) and link it from the contact page.

positives: Your case study numbers are genuinely impressive — 300% TikTok growth for Heinz, 30% CPA reduction for Somatic School, 120% brand awareness for Building Materials Nationwide. These are the sort of numbers that make a business owner stop scrolling.
Your testimonials are a masterclass — named clients, real businesses, some on video. Rai Atwal on camera saying 'the end result was fantastic' is worth more than a hundred written quotes.
Your Pricing page is refreshingly open — £400/day, £800/day, £600-2,000 retainers, all published. Most videographers hide this and lose enquiries because of it; you don't.
The £999 Hero Film on your Events page is a great productised offer — named, priced, specific deliverable. Exactly the clarity an SME owner wants before they enquire.
The logo strip of agency clients (NatWest, Heinz, Microsoft, TikTok) gives you credibility that most freelance videographers can't match — a real shortcut to 'this person is legit'.
